content.text: is an open-source repository of Jupyter notebooks that demonstrate practical Claude integration patterns. Each notebook is standalone and executable — clone the repo, install dependencies, add your API key, and run. The cookbook is maintained by Anthropic and regularly updated as new API features ship.
and is the recommended first stop when you want to understand how a specific capability works in practice, beyond what the reference documentation describes.

attrs.alt: Anthropic Cookbook repository structure — folders and key notebooks

content.text: The cookbook is organised by capability domain rather than by difficulty level. Each folder is self-contained:
Standalone recipes: token counting, embeddings, citations, caching
Vision (image input), PDF processing, audio transcription
Function calling: calculator, web search, SQL, parallel tools
Agentic loops, research agents, code execution, multi-agent
LangChain, LlamaIndex, Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock integrations
Evaluation harnesses: prompt comparison, output scoring

attrs.alt: Recommended learning path from basic API usage through to production patterns

content.text:
# Clone and set up
git clone https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook.git
cd anthropic-cookbook

# Install dependencies (each folder may have its own requirements)
pip install anthropic jupyter

# Set your API key
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-api03-..."

# Launch Jupyter
jupyter lab
Some notebooks have conflicting dependency versions. Create a separate venv or conda environment per notebook folder rather than installing everything globally.
The entry point for the cookbook. Covers messages API structure, system prompts, multi-turn conversations, and streaming. If you haven't called the API before, start here.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()   # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

# Basic call
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain token budgeting in one paragraph."}]
)
print(message.content[0].text)

# Streaming (for responsive UIs)
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku about APIs."}]
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)

Demonstrates how to define tools, parse tool-use responses, execute tools, and return results in a loop. The calculator example is the simplest starting point before tackling complex multi-tool scenarios.

parameter to cache large system prompts, reducing latency and cost on repeated calls that share a long prompt prefix. Essential for production deployments with large knowledge documents in the context.

# Mark a large document as cacheable — first call primes the cache
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=[{
        "type": "text",
        "text": large_document_text,
        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}   # cache this prefix
    }],
    messages=[{"role": "user",
               "content": "Summarise the key financial risks."}]
)
# Subsequent calls with the same system content hit the cache
# — dramatically lower latency for the cached tokens
Demonstrates base64 image encoding, multi-image inputs, and extracting structured information from images using tool use.

The most complete example in the cookbook. Implements a multi-step research agent that: plans a research strategy, performs web searches using a tool, reads and summarises sources, and compiles a final report. Use it as a reference architecture for any agentic workflow.
Planning via chain-of-thought before tool calls, handling tool failures gracefully, synthesising results across multiple tool calls, and bounding the agentic loop with a max-steps limit.
All cookbook notebooks can be converted to plain Python scripts with
, making them suitable for CI pipelines or running headlessly on a server:

# Convert a notebook to a Python script
jupyter nbconvert --to script tool_use/calculator_tool.ipynb
python tool_use/calculator_tool.py

# Execute a notebook non-interactively (outputs stored in the .ipynb)
jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute tool_use/calculator_tool.ipynb

The cookbook accepts community contributions. If you have a well-tested pattern that fills a gap not covered by existing notebooks, open a pull request on GitHub. Contribution guidelines are in the repo's CONTRIBUTING.md file.
Each notebook must run without external dependencies beyond what's pip-installable.
Demonstrate one concept per notebook. Breadth notebooks are split, not merged.
Never commit API keys — the notebook reads
Run the notebook top-to-bottom in a clean virtual environment.
Using the Cookbook Alongside the Docs
See working code for a pattern
API reference error codes + Cookbook error-handling patterns
Claude can request multiple tool calls in a single response when they can be executed independently. The
notebook demonstrates how to execute them concurrently and return all results in a single

import anthropic, concurrent.futures

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def execute_tool(name: str, inputs: dict) -> str:
    if name == "get_weather":
        return f"Weather in {inputs['city']}: 22C, sunny"
    if name == "get_population":
        return f"Population of {inputs['city']}: 1.2M"
    return "Unknown tool"

messages = [{"role": "user",
             "content": "Get the weather and population of Paris simultaneously."}]

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[
        {"name": "get_weather",
         "description": "Get current weather for a city.",
         "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                          "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
                          "required": ["city"]}},
        {"name": "get_population",
         "description": "Get current population of a city.",
         "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                          "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
                          "required": ["city"]}},
    ],
    messages=messages
)

# Execute all tool calls in parallel
tool_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor() as pool:
    futures = {pool.submit(execute_tool, b.name, b.input): b for b in tool_blocks}
    tool_results = [
        {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": b.id,
         "content": futures[f].result()}
        for f, b in [(f, futures[f]) for f in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures)]
    ]

messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

final = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[], messages=messages
)
print(final.content[0].text)
Pass PDFs directly to the API using the
content block type — Claude reads the full text and any embedded images. Useful for contract review, report summarisation, and form extraction without a separate OCR pipeline.
Force JSON-schema-conformant output by defining a single tool with the desired schema and telling Claude to always call it. More reliable than asking for JSON in the prompt.
Evaluating Your Prompts with the Cookbook Evals Framework

folder contains a lightweight harness for comparing prompt variants across a test set. This is the right tool when you want to answer "is prompt B actually better than prompt A?" with evidence rather than intuition.

# evals/basic_eval.py (simplified from the cookbook pattern)
import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TEST_CASES = [
    {"input": "Summarise in one sentence: The sky is blue...",
     "expected_keywords": ["sky", "blue"]},
    {"input": "Translate to French: Hello",
     "expected_keywords": ["Bonjour"]},
]

def run_eval(system_prompt: str, test_cases: list) -> float:
    passed = 0
    for case in test_cases:
        r = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=256,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}]
        )
        output = r.content[0].text.lower()
        if all(kw.lower() in output for kw in case["expected_keywords"]):
            passed += 1
    return passed / len(test_cases)

score_a = run_eval("You are a helpful assistant.", TEST_CASES)
score_b = run_eval("You are a precise, concise assistant.", TEST_CASES)
print(f"Prompt A: {score_a:.0%}  Prompt B: {score_b:.0%}")
Before any prompt change ships to production, run the eval suite. Even small wording changes can meaningfully shift output quality — evals make this visible.
Click Watch → Custom → Releases on GitHub to get notified when new notebooks are added.
Anthropic maintains a changelog noting which new API features have cookbook examples.
New cookbook notebooks are often announced in developer-focused blog posts alongside API launches.
If you vendor a cookbook notebook into your codebase, pin the commit hash — the cookbook evolves and may have breaking interface changes between notebook versions.
The Anthropic Cookbook is the practical counterpart to the API reference — use it to see real, runnable implementations of every major Claude capability. Start with
to confirm your setup, advance through
to learn function calling, and use
as the reference architecture for any agentic workflow. Each notebook is self-contained, executable, and kept current with API changes — clone the repo, set
